evals.prompt:
You are determining the design CBR and subgrade class for a Uganda trunk road project: the Jinja-Iganga road upgrade (UNRA). The road section is 15 km long (Ch 0+000 to Ch 15+000). The region has annual rainfall of 1400 mm with two wet seasons. Soil classification (Skill #8) indicates lateritic sandy clay (AASHTO A-6) throughout. The water table is approximately 2 m below the proposed formation level.

8 CBR test results (soaked 4-day, compacted at OMC to 100% Proctor MDD per MoWT Table 3.2):

| Test | Chainage | CBR (%) |
|------|----------|--------|
| 1 | 1+000 | 7 |
| 2 | 3+000 | 10 |
| 3 | 5+000 | 12 |
| 4 | 7+000 | 8 |
| 5 | 9+000 | 15 |
| 6 | 10+500 | 9 |
| 7 | 12+000 | 11 |
| 8 | 14+000 | 14 |

The design traffic class is T5 (3-6 million ESAs). Expected drainage provision is good (new construction with side drains). Expected maintenance level is uncertain.

Determine the design CBR, subgrade class, wet/dry design condition, and minimum investigation depth. Provide the complete subgrade characterization output.
evals.expected_output: Design CBR should be determined using the 10th percentile method per MoWT Section 3.3. Ranked values: 7, 8, 9, 10, 11, 12, 14, 15. With 8 results, the 10th percentile is approximated by the lowest value (7%) or interpolation giving approximately 7-8%. Design CBR = 7% or 8%. Subgrade class per Table 3.1: CBR 7% = S3 (5-7%); CBR 8% = S4 (8-14%). This is a boundary case requiring careful assessment. Mean = 10.75, SD = 2.8, statistical 10th percentile = 10.75 - 1.28*2.8 = 7.2% -> S3. Wet/dry condition: rainfall 1400mm = wet; per Table 4.1, good drainage + uncertain maintenance + high traffic (T5) -> W (wet catalogue). Minimum subgrade depth per Table 3.3: S3 = 350 mm, S4 = 450 mm.

assertions.text: Applies the 10th percentile method per MoWT Section 3.3 and determines design CBR in the range 7-8%
Identifies this as a boundary case between S3 (CBR 5-7%) and S4 (CBR 8-14%) per Table 3.1, and recommends the conservative classification S3 or discusses the boundary explicitly
Correctly selects wet design condition (W catalogue) per Table 4.1 based on rainfall 1400mm, good drainage, uncertain maintenance, and high traffic (T5)
States minimum subgrade depth from Table 3.3 appropriate for the assigned class (S3=350mm or S4=450mm)
Confirms CBR test conditions are correct per MoWT Table 3.2 (soaked 4-day, 100% Proctor MDD) for wet conditions

evals.prompt:
You are determining the subgrade foundation class for a UK road improvement project: a new housing estate access road in Warwickshire. The subgrade is Lias Clay. The water table is 2.5 m below formation level.

6 equilibrium CBR estimates from plasticity data and confirmed by 3 in-situ plate bearing tests:

| Test | Method | CBR (%) |
|------|--------|---------|
| 1 | PI correlation | 3.5 |
| 2 | PI correlation | 4.0 |
| 3 | PI correlation | 3.2 |
| 4 | Plate bearing | 4.5 |
| 5 | Plate bearing | 3.8 |
| 6 | Plate bearing | 5.0 |

Determine the foundation class per CD 225 and recommend the foundation treatment. Address frost susceptibility.
evals.expected_output: Design equilibrium CBR approximately 3.2-3.5% (10th percentile from ranked values). Foundation Class 2 (CBR 2.5-5%). Capping layer required: minimum 350mm Class 6F2 or equivalent, plus sub-base. Lias Clay: PI typically 30-50%, generally non-frost-susceptible due to high clay content and low permeability. Note that UK uses equilibrium CBR, not soaked CBR.

assertions.text: Assigns Foundation Class 2 (CBR 2.5-5%) based on the equilibrium CBR values
Recommends capping layer per CD 225 for Foundation Class 2 subgrade
Addresses frost susceptibility and concludes Lias Clay is generally non-frost-susceptible due to high PI/clay content
Notes the use of equilibrium CBR (not soaked CBR) as appropriate for UK design per CD 225

evals.prompt:
You are determining the design CBR for a Uganda district road project. The road is 8 km long. Due to budget constraints, only 2 CBR tests were performed:

| Test | Chainage | CBR (soaked, 100% T-99 MDD) |
|------|----------|----|
| 1 | 2+000 | 6 |
| 2 | 6+000 | 11 |

The project stage is detailed design. The traffic class is T2 (0.3-0.7 million ESAs). Rainfall is approximately 1100 mm/year.

Determine the design CBR and subgrade class. Identify all issues with the data and provide recommendations.
evals.expected_output: Only 2 CBR results are available -- well below the MoWT Table 3.2 minimum of 6 per uniform section. Statistical classification is not possible with 2 results. Must use the lower value (CBR 6%) conservatively. The two values (6% and 11%) are quite different -- the section may not be uniform. Recommend additional testing: at least 4 more CBR tests to reach the minimum of 6. Conservatively, design CBR = 6%, subgrade class = S3 (5-7%). Escalation flag for insufficient data. Despite being a low-traffic road (T2), the standard for data quality should not be relaxed -- the cost of additional testing is minimal compared to pavement construction cost.

assertions.text: Flags that only 2 CBR results are insufficient -- MoWT Table 3.2 requires minimum 6 representative samples per uniform section
Uses the lower CBR value (6%) conservatively as the design CBR, since statistical methods are unreliable with only 2 data points
Recommends additional CBR testing (at least 4 more tests to reach minimum 6) before finalising the design
Raises an escalation flag for insufficient data for reliable subgrade classification
Notes that the large difference between the two values (6% vs 11%) suggests the section may not be uniform and section delineation should be investigated

evals.prompt: Characterize the subgrade for a 25 km Class D road in Western Kenya. DCP survey results show CBR values: 8, 12, 6, 15, 9, 11, 7, 14, 10, 8. The region has high rainfall (>1200 mm/year). Determine the design subgrade class using the Kenya method and identify any issues.
evals.expected_output: Use Kenya RDM 3.3 Table 4.1 subgrade classes (NOT Uganda S1-S6 boundaries). Apply CUSUM method (Section 4.3.1) for section identification. Mean CBR = 10.0%. CUSUM shows no clear inflection -- treat as single section. Average 10.0% equals S3 median (10). But minimum CBR = 6% is below S3 lower bound of 7% -- the minimum value check fails. Either reclassify to S2 (requires capping per S3 minimum rule for paved roads) or isolate the weak point for localised treatment. S3 is the minimum subgrade for paved road foundations per RDM 3.3 Section 3.4.

assertions.text: Uses Kenya subgrade classes from RDM 3.3 Table 4.1 (not Uganda S1-S6 boundaries), with Kenya class ranges such as S3: CBR 7-13%
Applies the CUSUM method or average-based design CBR selection method from RDM 3.3 Section 4.3, not the 10th percentile method
Identifies that the minimum CBR value (6%) falls below the S3 class lower bound (7%) and flags this as an issue requiring resolution
Notes that S3 is the minimum subgrade class for paved road foundations per Kenya RDM 3.3 Section 3.4 (climate resilience measure)

evals.prompt: An ERA trunk road project in Ethiopia has 15 subgrade CBR test results along a 20 km section: 8, 12, 6, 9, 14, 7, 11, 5, 10, 8, 13, 7, 9, 6, 11. Using the ERA Flexible Pavement Design Manual Vol I 2013 Chapter 3, determine the design CBR using the 10th percentile method and the subgrade class from ERA Table 3-1.
evals.expected_output: Per ERA Pavement Vol I Chapter 3: sort CBR values ascending: 5, 6, 6, 7, 7, 8, 8, 9, 9, 10, 11, 11, 12, 13, 14. 10th percentile (lower) from 15 samples: approximately 6%. Design CBR = 6%. Per ERA Table 3-1: CBR 6% falls in subgrade class S2 (CBR 5-7%). ERA uses 10th percentile method (same as Uganda/TRL ORN 31), not Kenya's CUSUM average-based method. ERA has 5 subgrade classes (S1-S5), not 6 as in Uganda and Kenya.

assertions.text: References ERA Pavement Vol I 2013 Chapter 3 for subgrade characterization (not Uganda MoWT or Kenya RDM)
Uses 10th percentile method to determine design CBR (ERA/TRL ORN 31 approach)
Design CBR is approximately 6% from the 10th percentile of the dataset
Classifies subgrade as S2 (CBR 5-7%) per ERA Table 3-1 with five classes S1-S5
